data.title: EPHE: State Of Emergency, All Of ASEAN Exposed To Middle East Oil Supply
data.description:
The iShares MSCI Philippines ETF (EPHE) is a pretty interesting ETF. PE ratios are pretty low here for an economy that is probably above average as far as emerging economy profiles go. There is quite a lot of indexation to a port operator in the ETF which does index the ETF quite a bit to the intensity of trade with trade partners, which comes down to the important economic ties with China that do quite a lot to determine the Philippines' economic health. Other than that exposure, we are looking at a considerable financial exposure too that is probably not enjoying the market pressures and economic threats from the Iran war, which matter for Southeast Asia and China, but at least the yield curve is steepening, which should be good for the corporate books and the consumer books driven by things like car loans. It's a dragging market with a decent development profile and therefore could be primed to grow, but it's quite linked to Chinese fortunes, like much of the rest of SE Asia, and it has traded sideways consistent with the challenged deleveraging of China's economy. We think that the economic consequences of the Iran war may slow down China's recovery, but deleveraging for almost half a decade now, it's also about time that it starts recovering—some green shoots were showing. We think that will then lift the Philippines as well, and starting from a low valuation base, it may be a good way to ride those coattails as an ASEAN play.

But with oil dependence on the Middle East themselves, Philippines is exposed, which matters a lot for the industrial and financial exposures and also for the peso, in which their flows are denominated. It has declared a state of national emergency as well, with oil not that shored up, and planes could be grounded and oil generally unavailable.

EPHE Breakdown

Bear Case

The macro situation is that the Iran war poses serious threats to quite a few Southeast Asian countries and China, who are all very dependent on oil imports, specifically from the Middle East and through the Strait of Hormuz. The Philippines is in a state of emergency currently with guaranteed oil supply only going for a little over a month, thereafter depending on sputtering flows from the Middle East, where much of the oil comes from. The macro concern is pretty obvious. Higher oil prices threaten corporate profits and risk demand destruction. China had started to see industrial profit recovery, and this will slow it. The Philippines depends on Chinese export and import markets and its economic integration with China, as the rest of the ASEAN economies, depends as much on their domestic businesses as it does on China's, which has a lot of regional gravity. Higher oil prices are also a reinflation risk. These are all difficult-to-mitigate issues for the sectoral exposures in EPHE:

Sectorally, there is significant financial exposure and therefore quite a lot of exposure to full-service banks whose fortunes depend on good macro conditions. The pains of poor conditions show up in higher provisioning as delinquencies and credit issues increase. For foreign investors, the businesses are denominated locally, and therefore the FX matters. The Philippine Peso is not doing well, and the Philippines stock benchmarks are taking a hit as markets see a flight to safety occur, with the USD gaining after a poor streak on the debasement trade. For financials, the primary mitigant of the economic threat is that, at least with growth in categories like car loans and also business loans, they will be able to benefit from a steepening of the yield curve, with a considerably higher short- to medium-term rate profile than before due to the paused rate hike. The reasoning of the off-cycle central bank meeting and the indication of paused cuts is explicitly on account of the higher inflation and reinflation risks from the war.

For the industrial exposure, International Container Terminal Services (ICTEF) is 21.4% of the portfolio, which is a considerable skew to that specific holding. Half of the industrial exposure is accounted for by this stock. The concern here is general economic activity and the intensity of regional activity in a moment where oil prices, a key input, are becoming expensive or even somewhat unavailable. Countries like Japan, China, Hong Kong, and South Korea are all dependent on oil imports. These countries account for 40%+ of exports and imports. The higher prices per barrel, denominated in USD, also mean to meet needs more domestic currencies need to be sold, which is a downward vector on most of the ASEAN FX. While the port's business is infrastructural and altogether quite attractive, it is at the very least a growth risk, reflecting another disappointment in growth that followed the effects of the Liberation Day tariffs that threatened quite an important economic relationship with the US.

Other significant exposures are real estate, whose values are also dependent on a decent economy and would benefit from rate cuts, not likely in the current environment. The reinflation risks preclude any rate help, and real estate in general is going to a pro-cyclical category.

Bull Case

A steepening yield curve is good for a business that pays for shorter-term deposits to lend at longer-term rates - it lifts net interest margins. With a growth skew in the consumer credit world, the steepening in shorter horizons transmits the opportunity also to shorter-term credit, even credit card loans. The revision in short-term rates for the Philippines is particularly steep. This can help at least compensate the banking exposures for the higher risks.

The rest of the bull case rests on geopolitical hopes. Perhaps enough ships can pass through Hormuz despite the dangers and perhaps even be permitted by Iran to alleviate some of the acute supply risks. The oil futures curve is anticipating a normalization this year as well. Perhaps the war ends.

The market is also quite cheap in absolute and relative terms. With a Chinese recovery overdue, it's just a matter of time until growth gives more oxygen to the best in the region.

Bottom Line

Prior to this, compressed valuations, specifically the 9.6x PE (Indonesia, represented by (EIDO), is around 12x), relative to historical and other ASEAN nation levels, all meant there could be a value angle in the recovery. ASEAN is generally exposed to China, so as a recovery eventually plays out, it would be best to go with the cheapest market available. But the Iran war, where oil futures still show a late 2026 normalization, threatens these economies and their currencies enough that the hazard is quite real. There isn't really any sign that the war is ending soon, and oil continues to climb. The other issue we should mention is that corruption scandals have been hitting the country recently and are a source of quite a lot of concern as it relates to critical infrastructure in a disaster prone country. This is an FDI threat and therefore an FX threat, and a concrete impairment to the Philippines economy, as the magnitude of the corruption could be quite significant. It's also, for the moment, a civil unrest risk. In terms of expense ratio, it has one in line with EIDO which reflects a relatively good service for a pretty exotic ETF. But in all, these are quite hard-hit economies; the currency is marginal and the selling pressures considerable, with meaningful supply shocks that could start slowing down the intensity of trade for the industrial exposures and weaken financial and real estate bets. This isn't risk free even if better compensated relatively in the region due to the valuation case.

data.title: The Greatest Risk To Your Portfolio Is You
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Investing is easier than ever. You can access your brokerage account, transfer some funds, type in a ticker, and buy X shares in ACME Corp.

Investing is harder than ever. You log into your phone, and the stock market is down! The news is saying that things are going to get a lot worse. High energy costs, war, a declining labor market, and private credit collapsing, and we haven't even gotten to the bad news yet. They call it "doom scrolling" for a reason. And you can find a ton of reasons not to invest right now.

Technology has made investing more accessible to the average person. It is easier than ever for a person to start investing, and it can be done with a lower amount of initial capital. Investment strategies like options used to be the purview of sophisticated Wall Street professionals. Today, it's a 20-something on Robinhood who just checked a box agreeing that they are sophisticated.

While technology has increased the ease and availability of investing, it has increased the risks. The greatest risk of them all? Take a look in the mirror.

Let's look at the biggest mistakes investors make.

1. Failing To Recognize That The Stock Market Is Volatile

Stock prices are volatile. It isn't uncommon for the stock market to move 1%-2% in a day. Over longer periods, it isn't unusual for the stock market to sell off by large amounts.

Consider someone who invested $1 million in 1993, an amount of money that back then was widely regarded as life-changing money. And 1993 was a great time to invest.

The investor saw their balance run up over $3.2 million! Party on, Wayne!

Then over the next three years, the investor saw their principal cut in half. Then another rally back up, only to be cut in half again.

In the abstract, it is easy to understand that equities can and do decline 50% sometimes. We can look at a chart today, and those dips look small and like no big deal:

Yet, the psychological reality is that it was a decade of zero returns and worse, for much of 2000-2010, stocks were down a lot. Investors, by and large, didn't just hold through it. They sold. That's why the S&P 500 index went down, because people were more eager to sell than they were to buy.

At the end of the day, whatever number is represented by 30% of your portfolio is probably "a lot" of money to you.

The S&P 500 has been on a historic run recently. Sure, there was that dip during COVID, and there was a mild bear market in 2022, but despite those speed bumps that proved relatively short-lived, the S&P 500 is closing in on one of its most profitable periods ever.

Over the past 15 years, the S&P 500 has had a CAGR of approximately 13%. That is well above its average, which, since 1985, the average 15-year return for the S&P 500 has been 9.45%, with a low of 3.64%:

The performance we've seen over the past 15 years has been stronger than usual. Unfortunately, the result seems to be that many investors today haven't known anything different, and they feel invincible.

What happens when they find out differently? The risk is that they panic and sell.

2. Putting Everything Into Equities

Buying common equities has become relatively easy. Buying bonds and other debt investments is much more difficult. The result is that many investors have portfolios that are primarily invested in equities. Often, we will discuss preferred equities, bonds, or funds that invest in debt, and we will get feedback along the lines of:

"Why would I invest in that? It has dramatically underperformed the S&P 500!"

This is especially true if you're looking back from today, where debt investments saw substantial sell-offs due to rising interest rates. The recent performance of bonds has been poor relative to equities.

In terms of total return, equities have more upside potential. They always have, and they always will have more upside potential. This is because equities take on greater risks.

Taking on more risk is wonderful if you are going through a period where equities are having higher-than-average returns. Yet as we discussed above, these returns are neither typical nor guaranteed to continue.

Having a portion of your portfolio dedicated to debt isn't going to produce the best results in most years. However, it will decrease your volatility and, more importantly, improve the cash flow of your portfolio, ensuring that you aren't forced to sell your equities at low prices.

We recommend holding a variety of investments beyond just equities, including traditional bonds, baby bonds, preferred equity, bond funds, and companies that invest in debt like mortgage REITs.

3. Not Having A Plan

Too many investors go into the market without a plan. They buy stocks but don't really have an underlying strategy. When they read something positive about a stock, they buy it. When they read something negative, they sell it. Often finding themselves buying high when stocks are popular and selling low when they are unpopular.

The result is that they often find themselves chasing what is popular today, jumping into crowded trades, and always being late. There are investment strategies that seek to profit from the ebbs and flows of popularity. If you are going to try to do that, you need to make sure that you are ahead of the crowd, not behind it.

The end result of not having a plan is that when met with market turmoil, you will sell.

The Solution

Regular readers of mine know what our solution is. We call it the "Income Method", and it involves investing in investments with a focus on cash flow. This strategy helps resolve the problems above. How?

  1. Our focus is on the cash flow that our portfolio is producing. As a result, the day-to-day volatility of prices doesn't play a major role in our decision-making. The income we withdraw from the portfolio is funded by interest and dividends, not relying on selling at high prices. In fact, we look forward to opportunities to reinvest a portion of our dividends at lower prices.
  2. As an income-centric strategy, we naturally are biased toward investment classes that produce interest or dividends. Fixed-income and debt-focused funds are holdings that produce cash flow.
  3. We have a plan that accounts for the types of investments we will buy, the yield we are targeting, and our goal of 8%-10% current yield. Our plan includes guardrails like allocation limits and diversification.

Is that the only possible plan? Of course not. Investing is an open slate that allows investors to make money using numerous different strategies. However, it is crucial to make sure your strategy considers the volatility of the market, diversifies beyond just common equities, and that you are pursuing a plan that you are capable of executing.

Conclusion

Volatility is picking up in the market, and we're seeing the market reacting to global events, which by definition are unpredictable. Will the market crash? Absolutely. It is an inevitable reality that is the equivalent of asking whether it will snow in Alaska. Will it happen in 2026? That is the harder question to answer.

It is important for us to recognize that equities are volatile and that wild price swings aren't something to be feared. They should be expected, and investors need to approach the market with a healthy mindset to deal with the mental stress of those swings.

Part of that is having assets that are outside common equities. We like to hold bonds, baby bonds, preferred equities, and debt funds.

Ultimately, it comes down to having a plan that is going to account for the realities of the market, while also accounting for your personal emotions. Many investors panic, they see red, and they sell. Realizing losses when they should be looking for opportunities to buy.

The greatest barrier to your investing success is looking at you in the mirror. If you can handle your emotions and make principled decisions in the face of adversity, you will discover investing success.

data.title: Zurn Elkay Water Solutions' Fundamentals Shine, But The Stock Is Running Hot
data.description:
Whenever I write a company a 'hold', I am making the claim that the stock should perform more or less along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future. One business that has fallen a bit short of expectations when it comes to this is Zurn Elkay Water Solutions (ZWS). Conceptually, this is a fascinating business. Or at least, it is to me. For those not familiar, it essentially operates as a pureplay water management enterprise that provides customers with a portfolio of products such as professional grade water safety and control offerings, flow systems, environmental products, filtered drinking water products, hygienic products, and more. But even though it operates in an interesting space, the stock has risen only 1.3% since I last wrote about it. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up 5.2% over that same window of time.

Fundamentally speaking, Zurn Elkay Water Solutions is actually doing really well right now. Management continues to grow revenue, profits, and cash flows. The problem with the enterprise is that shares are anything but cheap. If it weren't for the space that the business is in and its overall financial performance over time, I would actually be a bit bearish about it. For now, however, I think that assigning it a 'hold' rating is logical. However, it wouldn't take much for me to downgrade it.

Shares aren't ready for a downgrade… yet

Whenever I analyze a publicly traded company, I typically like to start with the most recent data that management has offered up. In the case of Zurn Elkay Water Solutions, this would involve financial results for the last quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the business was $407.2 million. That happens to be 9.8% above the $370.7 million that the company reported a year earlier. This jump in revenue is fantastic to see. And according to management, it was driven by core sales growth of about 10%, with almost every product category boosting sales higher.

As revenue for the company increased, profitability followed suit. Net profits increased from $36.4 million to $42.1 million. On an adjusted basis, the increase was from $56.6 million to $61.6 million. Other profitability metrics rose without exception. We saw an increase in operating cash flow from $63.6 million to $91.7 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, the rise would be from $73 million to $88.5 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business popped from $91.1 million to $104.1 million.

The chart above shows financial results for 2025 compared to 2024. As was the case for the final quarter, we saw revenue, profits, and cash flows, all increased for the enterprise. When you dig into the data deeper, you get some insight into why revenue and profitability seem to be expanding. For the year as a whole, for instance, the company saw strong performance in the US. Revenue of $1.52 billion ended up being 9% above what it was a year earlier. And in Canada, sales jumped 4% from $145.8 million to $151.7 million.

Management mentioned that non-residential end markets were the strongest for it. Considering what is going on in the residential space, with home prices and home construction activity worsening, this doesn't surprise me. We seem to be seeing good growth when it comes to institutional customers and waterworks businesses. Examples of these would be schools, companies in general infrastructure, and the commercial plumbing space. As great as it is to see revenue rise, it is worth noting that around 5 percentage points of sales increase for the company came from tariff related pricing actions that management had to take. This means that essentially pushed those higher costs onto its customers. But the fact that overall profitability for the company improved as well means that management has been successful in preserving margins.

Revenue for 2026 is likely to continue growing if management turns out to be accurate. They expect core revenue growth to be in a mid-single-digit range for the year. The continued benefit of higher prices should help the business. However, as the year progresses, they expect higher volume to help them. Unfortunately, they expect that the residential market will remain weak. The commercial space should even be more or less flat. But the firm does expect a low single digit growth rate when it comes to the institutional and waterworks end markets.

You also have continued tailwinds when it comes to the water and filtration space, especially as regulators push to address PFAS (or what are known as 'forever chemicals). Management even introduced, during the quarter, a filter that expanded filtration beyond just PFOA and PFOS in order to capture the full family of these harmful chemicals. These new filters have been certified to reduce microplastics, lead, and other harmful contaminants. Plus, the company continues to push out other offerings, such as top mount filter access for its Pro Filtration product line, as well as its Liv EZ product line that should bring commercial grade water filtration into residences, commercial properties, and hospitals. Add on top of this the fact that the company launched a recycling program for used filters with TerraCycle, and there is a lot to be optimistic about from an operational standpoint.

Management is doing more than just focus on water. It also has its own hand dryer platform called World Dryer. In 2025 alone, these devices are eliminated, according to management, the need for 3.5 billion paper towels. The firm also launched, during the year, its SANITIZE + DRY sanitizing dryer, which they described as a breakthrough hygienic, sustainable hand dryer. Using cold plasma technology, it supposedly neutralizes 99.99% of common bacteria and viruses, including COVID. These types of innovations should bode well for investors in the long run.

According to management, EBITDA margin for 2026 should be around 35%. Assuming a 5% revenue growth rate for the business, we would be looking at EBITDA for the year of $458.5 million. That is a bit above the $442.2 million that the company reported for last year. Assuming that other profitability metrics rise similarly, we would get adjusted net income of $270.4 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $332 million. If we use these estimates, we can see in the chart below that the company is actually trading at some pretty lofty multiples. For most companies in most industries, I would actually take a bearish stance here. However, when it comes to this market, I do think some additional wiggle room should be provided, especially when it comes to such a high-quality business that is continuing to expand even as market conditions remain challenging. Having said that, a downgrade probably is not too far into the future if things continue as they happen. As that chart also illustrates, the company is more expensive than four of the five firms that I stacked it up against in every respect. This is disconcerting to say the least.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available at this moment, it is my assertion that, on the whole, Zurn Elkay Water Solutions is actually a really solid business. But this does not mean that it makes sense for investors to buy into right now. The fact of the matter is that shares are rather pricey. In fact, because of how expensive they are, I think he downgraded for the company might not be too far into the future. For now, however, I remain more neutral, and as such, I have decided to maintain it as a 'hold' candidate.

data.title: COWZ: Makes It Easy To Navigate Market Selloffs
data.description:
Overview

Market indices continue to retreat from their prior highs with the continued selloff of SaaS companies and rising tensions with Iran. While the S&P 500 (SPX) and Nasdaq-100 (NDX) Indices have experienced a rough start for 2026, defensive ETFs like the Pacer US Cash Cows 100 ETF (COWZ) can offer a safe haven for investors looking to navigate the headwinds. When I previously covered COWZ, I issued a buy rating due to the defensive positioning of the fund. Since then, COWZ has outperformed the S&P 500 and has done a great job at protecting capital. It's been a year since my last coverage so I wanted to reassess the ETF's overall value proposition and outlook through 2026.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that COWZ's share price has increased by about 11.6%. When including all dividends that were paid out to shareholders, the total return rises to 15.1% over the same time frame. COWZ now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 2% at this time but the value isn't in the yield. The value may actually be in the dividend growth potential over a longer holding period.

Periods of market uncertainty and rapid declines always serve as a reminder for why capital preservation is important. This is why I wanted to also revisit COWZ to discuss why the ETF is a great option for investors approaching the retirement age. The transition from active to passive income can be challenging and sometimes investors chase higher yields to instantly get more cash flow. However, COWZ proves that patience can be rewarded with higher income over time. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's underlying fund strategy.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, COWZ now has total net assets of $18.1B that are spread across 102 different positions. The fund's process starts by screening the Russell 1000 Index in search for the top 100 companies based on their free cash flow yields. This is important to remember because a company that is generating more cash than it needs usually has a strong balance sheet, flexibility during unfavorable market cycles, and the ability to reinvest capital back into the growth of its business. This process almost eliminates companies that have negative earnings, which limits fluff to poor performers.

The fund has a reasonable expense ratio of 0.49%, which may be higher than standard index-tracking ETFs. However, this higher expense ratio may be worth it considering the filtering process that management implements to secure positions in the strongest companies possible. When looking at the performance of COWZ against the index, we can see that there are some periods of outperformance and underperformance. It really comes down to market conditions since it looks like COWZ will outperform the index during times of uncertainty, such as 2022's market decline related to interest rate fluctuations. The portfolio is rebalanced and reconstituted on a quarterly basis to ensure that the fund is always exposed to the holdings that best match its eligibility requirements. Tracking error can be minimal due to the rules based nature of the index.

Looking at the portfolio's breakdown by sector exposure, COWZ is now most heavily allocated to the health care sector at 21.79% of assets. The fund has made some substantial shifts over the last year in its portfolio allocation. For instance, COWZ was most heavily allocated to energy investments at the time of my last coverage. With that said, the allocation can constantly shift based on market cycles and this flexibility is great for a defensive approach.

When looking at the fund's top holdings, we can see that COWZ provides exposure to many high quality positions. COWZ specifically chooses to focus on large-cap companies that may be trading at a discount and have the potential for capital appreciation. As of the current data, COWZ's portfolio has a price to earnings ratio of 16.30x, which undercuts the Russell 1000's price to earnings ratio of ~26.15x, according to the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB).

The largest individual position is now ConocoPhillips (COP) at 2.34% of assets. This is followed by many other high quality names, including Pfizer (PFE), AT&T (T), Verizon Communications (VZ), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), and Altria Group (MO), just to name a few. Although it isn't specifically stated, the focus on companies with a solid free cash flow typically means that COWZ will have exposure to companies with a history of solid dividend growth.

However, the fund's current structure is likely to underperformance against market indices during strong bull markets. Since the fund doesn't maintain exposure to growth companies that do not pay dividends, the fund's ability to provide capital appreciation may not satisfy some investors. However, the fund's success shouldn't be measured by its ability to keep up with the S&P 500. Instead, the success should be measured by its ability to protect capital over longer stretches of time, while also providing favorable total returns.

Outlook: Ability To Preserve Capital

With tensions related to the Strait of Hormuz rising, an uptick in unemployment, high interest rates, and a selloff in technology, investors have experienced a rough start to 2026. For instance, the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has now experienced a total loss of nearly 2% over the last six months. However, COWZ has been able to efficiently grow capital and outperform with a total return of 9.9% over the same time frame. The key is that companies with impressive free cash flow levels means that their valuation floors are typically a lot more sturdy than rapid growth companies.

Interestingly, the fund has proven to capture a lower percentage of the market's downside movements. When management completed a back test over the last 24 years, COWZ only captured 88% of the downside movement. So in a scenario where the S&P 500 were to decline by 10%, COWZ is statistically likely to only decline by 8.8% over the same time frame. This may not seem like a bit difference on a surface level, but it can certainly provide a nice buffer for retirees that want to protect capital. This resilience may even be attributed to the fact that companies with large free cash flow yields have the possibility to implement buybacks during market dips.

With current geopolitical risks, the price of oil still hovers around the $100 per barrel mark. While this has put pressure on some sectors of the market, energy companies seem to be the main ones positioned for success. Despite the portfolio shifts since my last coverage, COWZ still has a sizeable allocation to energy companies. So if the global tension do not ease and oil remains high, I think COWZ is positioned to do well. Even if technology sells off, COW's shift into healthcare companies means the fund is taking a value approach since many healthcare companies have fallen to attractive multiples. For instance, Pfizer trades at a forward price to earnings of 12.48x, which undercuts the sector median of 22.53x.

COWZ has historically demonstrated that it is resistant to the higher interest rate environment. Part of the selection process is to avoid high leverage companies, which need to borrow capital to support operations. COWZ focuses on the opposite structure: companies that have strong free cash flows and are self-funding. So in a higher for longer interest rate environment, COWZ can effectively protect capital and still provide meaningful growth. So assuming that interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months, this should provide relief to the rest of the market and COWZ should be able to ride this positive momentum.

Dividend Growth

The latest declared quarterly dividend of $0.2003 per share brings the estimated dividend yield slightly above 2%. While the low dividend yield may not be that exciting, the primary benefit is the dividend growth potential when a position is maintained for a longer holding period. For instance, COWZ has been able to increase its dividends at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 8.68% over the last five years. The fund has also maintained a positive growth streak of four consecutive years, which is a powerful tool in an environment of rising inflation and costs of living.

Investors that maintain a long-term position in COWZ would see their annual dividend income rise and their yield on cost substantially increased. For instance, investors that maintained a position for the last ten years would now have a yield on cost of more than 5%. The beauty of focusing on companies with strong free cash flows is that COWZ is likely to provide continuous growth, even if market indices are trending lower. This is why COWZ is such a solid defensive position for investors with a low risk tolerance.

The sustainability of the yield is anchored by the underlying selection methodology. COWZ maintains exposure to companies with the highest free cash flow yields. Since dividends are paid out of free cash flow, the coverage rate is structurally superior to earnings based funds. Therefore, I would anticipate that COWZ is capable of averaging an increase CAGR between 5% to 7% going forward. Furthermore, the tilt towards healthcare companies will probably help with sustainability since this is an industry where spending is consistent during most market cycles.

So although the starting yield is low, COWZ is a great option for investors that have a low risk tolerance and may be nearing the retirement age. By accumulating COWZ a few years away from making the shift from active to passive income, investors would be allowing some time for the dividend growth and compounding effect to take place. Furthermore, COWZ's dividend growth means that investors can get a sizeable dividend yield without needing to take extra risk with debt-focused credit funds or REITs.

To help visualize this concept, I ran a back test of an initial $100,000 investment at the start of 2017. This back test assumes that no additional capital was invested after the initial $100k and it also assumes that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of more shares. By simply remaining invested, investors would have been able to more than double their annual dividend income over the last few years.

2017 Dividend Income: $2,308

2025 Dividend Income: $6,366

Lastly, the fund may surprisingly issue distributions that aren't tax-efficient. Since the fund rebalances quarterly, the fund may trigger short-term capital gains and use those to help support the distributions. For instance, the latest payout was labeled as being supported by ordinary income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in COWZ may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Underperformance Risks

Although the fund has exposure to technology companies, COWZ is still very likely to underperform ETFs that maintain growth-oriented positions. Since inception, COWZ has underperformed SPY and QQQ. Although there are some periods where COWZ returns outpaced the S&P 500, a scenario where AI continues to expand earnings will likely have a big role in the expansion of the indices. For instance, the size of the AI market is estimated to grow to $3.5T by 2033, representing a 30.6% CAGR.

However, the market continues to selloff software companies due to the threat of AI. Advances within AI have become so good that investors are now worried that the low cost and accessibility of different AI tools will threaten the moat that many software companies had. While we don't necessarily know how this will turn out, COWZ does have direct exposure to many of the companies that have sold off over the last few months. This includes companies like:

  • Salesforce (CRM)
  • Adobe (ADBE)
  • Workday (WDAY)
  • Cognizant (CTSH)
  • Qualcomm (QCOM)

Conversely, a scenario where technology companies lead another market rally can also cause COWZ to get left behind. If valuations across the technology sector rises, the fund may sell out of these positions. So there is a risk that COWZ is actively selling out of the winners during the rebalancing period just to keep positions aligned with its specific parameters. Furthermore, COWZ completely excludes financial exposure so in a scenario where that industry leads a market rally, COWZ is not structurally capable of participating in that expansion.

Despite its underperformance risks, the fund can still hold its own weight during bull cycles. Historically, COWZ has captured 97.32% of the upside market growth over time. So not only are you getting more protection with COWZ, but you're also participating in the growth. So on a positive note, this means that COWZ actually provides the potential to outperform during specific market conditions.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that COWZ remains a buy at this time. As market uncertainty rises, the ability to preserve capital becomes very important for investors. We don't know how much more the indices can decline and COWZ's approach has a proven track record of success. The fund has historically captured less downside movements, while still demonstrating its ability to participate in market expansions. This makes the fund a great fit within a retired investor's portfolio. Additionally, the focus on free cash flow means that COWZ provides exposure to high quality dividend growth companies, which means that the fund is likely to continue paying out a higher dividend over time.

data.title: Viant Technology Inc: Will This Beaten-Down Ad-Tech Stock Bounce Back? 3 Things To Know
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Viant Technology Inc. (NASDAQ:DSP) has remained under pressure and is down significantly from its 52-week high of $16.25, due to concerns related to macroeconomic uncertainty, tariff-related pressure on advertising budgets, and a broader downside pressure across ad-tech stocks. However, based on my in-depth analysis of the company's latest financials, it seems the company has potential for upside. The analysis highlighted its double-digit revenue growth supported by improving industry growth, expanding margins, strong free cash flow, and a healthy balance sheet with no debt. I believe the fundamentals of the business are intact and investors could see upside over the next few quarters with improving macroeconomic headwinds.

Business Overview

According to Grand View Research, over 80% of digital display ads are now traded programmatically. The company operates in AI-based programmatic advertising through a cloud-based platform. This platform enables marketers to run advertising campaigns across multiple digital channels, i.e. Connected TV (CTV), streaming audio, mobile and desktop. In a nutshell, the company helps brands show advertisements to the right audience using data and technology. Within programmatic advertising, Viant operates a demand-side platform (DSP), which means it helps brands buy ads automatically online. The most relevant peer is The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD), which also offers a DSP. Larger players like Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE) and Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) offer DSP capabilities as well.

Source: Key Players in Programmatic Advertising, Market Research Future

On the other side of the industry, there are supply-side platforms (SSP) which support websites and apps sell their advertising space to brands. ViantAI is the flagship product of the company which supports every step of programmatic advertising. The company also acquired IRIS.TV and Lockr to further enhance its programmatic advertising capabilities.

3 Things Investors Should Watch

1. Growth in Total Addressable Market:

Source: Grand View Research

According to Grand View Research, the global programmatic advertising platform market is projected to increase by a CAGR of 27.1% to $41.4 Bn by 2030 from $8.4 Bn in 2023. This is due to adoption of big data, advanced analytics, and ad tech innovations. Further, the growth will be driven by the demand side platform (DSP) in which Viant operates and has the potential to grab more market through its product offering. Moreover, the application of AI has been increasing in the broader digital advertising market. With the application of AI, brands are looking to automate campaign planning, participating in auctions of ad space and optimization. In such a situation, DSP platforms like Viant will have a strong advantage due to its ViantAI autonomous advertising platform. In a nutshell, the addressable market is large, expanding rapidly, and Viant is well positioned for potential upside in stock price.

2. Strong Revenue Growth and Margin Expansion:

Revenue Analysis:

Data in $Mn Dec 25 Dec 24 Dec 23 Dec 22
Revenue 344.2 289.2 222.9 197.2
Growth Rate 19% 30% 13% -

Data compiled by the author using data from company filings and Seeking Alpha.

The revenue growth has been clearly showing a strong uptrend with a 20% CAGR between Dec 25 to Dec 22. In Q4 2025, revenue increased by 22% year-over-year to $110 Mn, driven by an increase in demand from brands in the healthcare, business services, retail, and consumer goods industry verticals. The company generates 46% of revenue from CTV across the different digital channels. The CTV advertising is gaining traction due to a structural shift as consumers move towards streaming services. The advertising industry is impacted by the macroeconomic situation as the advertising budgets of brands depend on the associated growth of their products. However, Viant is sustaining a double-digit growth rate despite tariff-related pressure. The street consensus for FY 2027 is expecting double-digit revenue growth, supported by the growth in ViantAI and accelerating demand side spend on the platform by brands. Based on the historical performance of the company and the industry growth rate of 27.1% CAGR by 2030, I believe the company is on a path to a sustained strong growth rate.

Looking forward, we see an unprecedented opportunity to accelerate top and bottom-line growth, led by the recent launch of Outcomes, our fully autonomous AI Decisioning advertising solution." Tim Vanderhook, Co-Founder and CEO of Viant.

Operating Margin Expansion:

Adjusted EBITDA Margin over the last eight quarters sourced from Q4 2025 Company Presentation

The company is not only increasing its revenue at an accelerating pace, but also having strong margin expansion. For investors, this is an important data point for evaluating investment decisions as it is translating revenue growth into operating profitability. The 50% sequential growth in adjusted EBITDA of Q4 2025 with margin expanding by 800 basis points reflects the acceptance of its cloud-based DSP model.

3. Healthy Free Cash Flow and Strong Balance Sheet:

FCF trend sourced from Q4 2025 Company Presentation

FCF increased by 132% year-over-year to $28 Mn in Q4 2025, providing support for both strategic investments and capital returns. Further, the company has a healthy $191 Mn in cash with no debt outstanding as of Dec 25, which signifies a healthy balance sheet for a company. With a strong FCF and available cash, the company has the financial capability to pursue strategic acquisitions and return cash to shareholders through stock buybacks. The company has repurchased 4.8 Mn shares from May 2024 through March 2026 worth $59.6 Mn of Class A common stock, with $40.4 Mn remaining under the authorized repurchase program as of Mar 2026. The share repurchase shows management's confidence in the business and is important for investors to track such corporate actions.

Relative Valuation - Trading At Discount Despite Improving Financials

Peers EV/EBITDA (Forward)
Viant Technology Inc (NASDAQ:DSP) 3.06x
The Trade Desk, Inc. (NASDAQ:TTD) 7.26x

Source: Seeking Alpha

Based on the above analysis, Viant is trading at a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 3.06x, which is a significant discount compared to the peer average of 4.77x. At the peer average multiple of 4.77x, the implied value is higher than the current stock price of $10.75. The Trade Desk, which I consider the most relevant peer is trading at a premium of 7.26x. With a strong revenue growth rate, improving margins and a healthy balance sheet the investors are paying a discount multiple for Viant. I believe the stock has a potential upside and will catch with industry peers and The Trade Desk multiple. Further, 8 Wall Street analysts covering the company have a Strong Buy consensus rating with an average target price of approximately $17–$18, representing significant upside from current levels. However, I suggest investors should conduct their own analysis and understand the risk factors for any investment decision.

Investment Risk

1. The advertising industry is cyclical in nature. During recession, companies cut down on advertising budgets, which directly impacts Viant's revenue growth.

2. Google, Amazon, Meta and other large technology platforms with large data and distribution ad space reach are functioning in the programmatic advertising industry. Investors should understand the competitive landscape, technological advancement and any change in market share trends before any investment decision.

Final Thoughts

The company is improving financially with strong revenue growth, improving operating margins, strong FCF, and a healthy balance sheet. Further, it operates in an industry which is projected to grow at 27.1% CAGR through 2030. I believe the company has a potential upside driven by financial strength, product innovation, adoption of AI and inorganic growth through acquisitions. However, investors have to watch the current macroeconomic situation and its impact on advertising budgets, the adoption rate of AI, and the competitive landscape.

data.title: KRE: Valuations Are Declining, But May Not Correct Soon
data.description:
I have written articles about a number of regional banks on Seeking Alpha over the last year that are a part of the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE). The differences between those with healthy operations and those that are struggling are significant. However, most all of them started this year with a few basic expectations. First, the impact of the dreaded “fiscal cliff” for commercial real estate may not be as bad a previously feared. Second, inflation would decline slightly, or at worst, remain relatively consistent just above the Fed’s target of 2%. Third, more interest rate cuts are in the pipeline, even if perhaps there would not be as many as previously hoped.

The recent military actions in the Middle East have brought some new fears to the market, or perhaps they simply rushed them to the front of the line faster than they would have required on their own. An article that appeared on CNBC earlier today referenced the increasing odds that interest rates will be raised, rather than cut. This, along with rising energy costs, are all part of a broader macro picture that have brought the odds of a recession in the US in the next 12 months to almost 50%, according to Moody’s Analytics.

From looking at the chart for the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF, one can see that January’s optimism was knocked back before the actions in Iran were even on the radar. By February 6, KRE had started the year with a gain of 13.6%. Unfortunately, what the market giveth, the market hath taken away. However, regional banks have still been able to stay ahead of the broader market by almost 4%.

So where do we go from here? The next two quarters for regional banks will likely be a bumpy ride with continued pessimism in the short-term being tamped down by a continuation of merger activity that was a big part of 2025. Valuations for regional banks are declining, and in the long-term this will create more opportunity or investors in KRE. Things may get a little worse before they get better, however, and therefore my current rating for the regional banking index is a Hold.

ETF Overview

With almost $4 billion in assets under management, the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF is a passively managed fund that tracks the performance of the S&P Regional Banks Select Industry Index. This index tracks a subset of companies in the financial sector that excludes large-cap, national banks as well as brokerage firms, insurance companies, and pure play mortgage lenders. It also screens out micro-cap banks that do not meet liquidity or market cap requirements. The fund, like the index it follows, is rebalanced quarterly.

As of March 25, there were 150 holdings in KRE. The top 10 holdings are shown below. The index that the fund follows is a modified equal-weighted index so there is not a wide range of differences between the allocations for the stocks on this list. This investment approach also helps the fund avoid overexposure to certain larger regional banks that would dominate if the fund were market cap weighted.

The largest holding in the fund as of my writing of this analysis is Flagstar Bank (FLG). FLG, which recently received a credit upgrade from Fitch Ratings, has a weight of 1.57%. This is only seven basis points more than the last bank on the list, FNB Corp (FNB). The top 42 stocks in the fund by weight have an allocation of at least 1%. The smallest share of KRE belongs to Columbia Financial (CLBK), a $1.8 billion company that is currently going through a second step conversion as part of a merger with another regional bank.

A profile of the portfolio held by KRE is shown in the tables below. The average PE ratio for stocks held by the fund is deep in value territory at just 10.77. According to Morningstar, this is well below the average for the typical financial ETF. The typical price-to-book ratio of 1.1 for KRE-owned stocks is also below the financial average.

Small- and micro-cap stocks hold a large majority of the allocation in the fund with just over 88% of its total investment. Mid-cap companies add just 9.7%. The modified equal weighting for the index used by the fund shows its impact here.

KRE has a dividend yield of 2.44% over the last 12 months. Distributions are made quarterly with the most recent landing in investors’ accounts on March 25. Dividend growth has not been a great strength of KRE over the last five years as the compound average growth rate for payouts from the fund is just 1.72%.

The fund’s expense ratio of 0.35% is similar to that from other peer funds. Broader financial ETFs like the iShares US Financials ETF (IYF) are within just a few basis points in management fees. Some larger funds like the Vanguard Financial Index (VFH) and the State Street Financial Select Sector ETF (XLF) do have expense ratios below 0.10%.

Past Performance of KRE

Last year was a relatively good one for the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF. The fund is highly unlikely to produce returns like a large-cap growth fund, but KRE was able to post its second straight year of double-digit returns. However, over a longer period of time, the fund has struggled to keep up with the market as a whole and even with the broader financial sector. Since its inception, the fund has only rewarded investors with a gain of about 4.2% per year.

The chart below shows how the fund has compared to its sector and provides a greater understanding for the variability for the performance of regional banks. When compared to the State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF, KRE has outperformed noticeably in the short-term. Its performance in 2026 is over 10% better than XLF. Over the last 12 months, the difference is even greater in favor of KRE.

However, over longer periods of time, the diversification and the exposure to larger companies in the sector have paid off for XLF investors. The failure of multiple regional banks in 2023 certainly played a large role in this divergence between the two funds. In that year, KRE ended up in the 99th percentile of all US financial funds for its return of -7.58% during a year when the broader sector gained over 12.5%.
