Prospects for the First Half of 2026

Back in early February, I wrote an analysis of the entire financial sector for Seeking Alpha. As part of that article, I expressed optimism for KRE, in part, because of the increase in banking deals that had taken place in 2025. I also noted that bank valuations were becoming more dependent on the geographic footprint of the bank that was being acquired bank, as the South, especially Texas and the Carolinas were especially attractive for M&A activity. A favorable regulatory environment has many regional banks on a spending spree which has provided some tailwinds for their valuations. It has been challenging for me to find a regional bank to analyze on this site that is not undergoing or just wrapping up a deal with another bank. The time required to close a transaction can be as little as three to four months, in some cases.

The conflict in the Middle East has thrown some uncertainty in the market. The International Energy Agency has said the global economic impact from the war may be more significant than the oil shock from the 1970’s. Higher fuel prices and higher bond rates have put a pause on deal-making in many sectors, at least in the short-term. Goldman Sachs (GS) stated last week that it expects deal making to pick back up later this year, but when that shift of gears in the market will take place is unclear.

What is clear for now is that inflation is still a significant problem, and interest rates are not declining anytime soon. Mortgage rates have risen for four straight weeks after teasing buyers with the potential for sub-6% 30-year rates earlier in 2026. Sellers now outnumber buyers in the market by the widest margin since 2013, according to Redfin. Banks depend on consumer mortgages for roughly 20% of their loans, so when there are fewer home sales taking place, growth in these loans is even more challenging.

All of these variables cast greater doubts on the health of the US economy and the prospects for increased profitability for regional banks. Q4 2025 GDP was revised down to 0.7%, and inflation, interest rates, and consumer confidence are all reasons to expect only modest gains in activity in the first half of 2026. If interest rates stay higher for longer, regional banks will face pressure on their unrealized losses on their securities portfolios. Most regional banks have benefited from declining deposit costs over the last year, but a reversal in that area could create added pressure on net interest margin and ultimately their bottom lines.

Additional Risks to Consider

Some of the risks involved with an investment in KRE are similar to the risks for exposure to any small-cap stock fund. Small-cap companies may struggle more during downturns in the overall market with greater volatility. Investors may engage in a flight to safety that supports buying larger, more well-known names in the financial sector. The fund’s slant toward equal weight investing also keeps it from benefiting from the growth of larger players in the space. Many of these larger regional banks hold higher quality assets than their small-cap peers, and they also have more financial cushion to take on headwinds in the market that may have a more meaningful impact on smaller companies.

While my own concerns over CRE exposure for regional banks are not as great as they once were, there is still an overhang that exists over the market. Many regional banks have exposure to CRE equaling 50% of more of their loan portfolios. Office and retail CRE have proven to be especially problematic. Regions Financial (RF), a company that has a 0.22% weight in KRE, specifically mentioned office loans as a reason for charge-offs in the second half of 2025. M&T Bank (MTB), which has an allocation of 0.41%, recently announced plans to cut back on its office loans. In some areas of the country, vacancy rates for office space are higher than they were after the global financial crisis. All totaled, about $936 billion of CRE loans are expected to mature at some point this year with $59.5 billion of that dedicated to office CRE.

Morningstar gives KRE its lowest rating of one star for risk-adjusted returns. The fund has a risk score of 96. This makes it riskier than that the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) which has a risk score of just 82. Even in good times, this fund is most appropriate for investors who can withstand a greater chance for losses in the short-term.

The Bottom Line

In the first month of this year, a rising tide was lifting almost all of the boats in the regional banking space. The last seven weeks have done just the opposite. The market is becoming more selective in the companies it rewards. As I have mentioned in several of my recent articles on Seeking Alpha, I have raised my own standards for the individual stocks that I consider worth a buy in this environment.

The decline in the valuations for many of the stocks included in the KRE portfolio will create opportunity for investors down the road. However, the next several weeks are likely to be similar to those from mid-February and early March. Even the mention of a rate hike before the next rate cut shows the risks that do exist with small-cap financial stocks like those owned by KRE. I consider the fund to be a Hold at the current time.

data.title: Iran Is Noise, Artificial General Intelligence Is The Signal, Nvidia Is The Trade
data.description:
I am starting my coverage of Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) discussing the idea that we may have finally reached AGI (Artificial General Intelligence), as recently claimed by Nvidia’s CEO.

For readers that do not know me, I have been covering the impact of AI on markets since I started my analyst work on Seeking Alpha in early 2024. My coverage included:

  • The idea of large language models, or LLMs, being commodities and how AI houses such as OpenAI (OPENAI) are not likely to “win” the AI race.

  • How Palantir Technologies, Inc. (PLTR) has been the first (and best) company to figure out how to monetize AI.

  • How AGI could trigger a new tech bubble.

  • Different scenarios concerning how and to what extent AI disruptions in the job market will impact stocks and interest rates going forward.

For some time now, I have been arguing AI is evolving exponentially, following a long trend that started well before “AI” became a widespread term. The chart below outlines that trend, which I refer to as the “commoditization of AI models.” For decades, AI models have been becoming simultaneously cheaper and more powerful.

Today, I will explore whether we really are on the cusp of achieving AGI, and what the impact on markets and Nvidia, the most “prominent” AI stock, may be.

Have we actually achieved AGI? Frankly, it doesn’t matter

The chart below showcases AI models scores in the ARC-AGI-2 test. In simple words, ARC-AGI-2 is a reasoning benchmark testing whether AI can adapt to entirely new problems, making it one of the most honest measures of how close AI is to true general intelligence.

The most capable frontier models now comfortably beat the average human score of 60%. Does this mean AGI has been reached? Probably not. There is significant ongoing debate about what exactly AGI means.

To me, this debate is fascinating from a philosophical standpoint but carries little value in practical terms. In my opinion, what matters is ultimately whether AI can or cannot do a human’s job. Whether it is actually “conscious” or not is pointless to the discussion. And personally, I find there is increasing reason to believe AI is taking over.

Consider not only the performance of AI models in closed tests, but the recent launches of different features in AI models:

  • Perplexity launched “Computer,” a new way to use LLMs that is similar to using an actual team of humans. “Computer” is said to “reason, delegate, search, build, remember, code, and deliver.”

  • Anthropic (ANTHRO) recently allowed users to enable Claude, its model, to complete tasks, directly accessing apps on a user’s computer to do so.

  • OpenAI and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) have launched “Operator” and “Project Mariner,” agents that can browse websites, click buttons, and execute tasks directly inside a user's browser.

  • The concept of FDE (Forward Deployed Engineer) has become mainstream in B2B sales, with companies like Google, Anthropic, OpenAI all hiring these figures, whose role is to work directly on-site with clients to automate workflows using AI.

Disruption is near: AI houses are figuring out the “Application Layer”

AI models are very limited in what they can do, if restricted to the form of a chatbot. The real value in AI is not in having a nice chatbot, but rather in the so-called “application layer.” That is, where models become usable tools, interfaces, and integrated workflows.

This concept was showcased by Palantir’s CEO Alex Karp back in 2024, at a time when most attention was still focused on quickly evolving LLMs. There is no chance that Palantir is, in my view, the company that first figured out how to actually operate and monetize AI beyond chatbots.

I think “pure” AI houses such as OpenAI and Anthropic have now figured out they need to move up the value chain in order to thrive and survive. That’s why we are seeing more and more focus on new tools (such as FDEs, Computer, Mariner, etc.) rather than a sole focus on new, more powerful LLMs.

Macro perspective: what AGI could mean for the economy

An AGI breakthrough would have significant repercussions on the global economy. I find the current debate about AGI mostly focuses on whether or not “conscious” AI would reshape the job market by automating jobs. This is likely true in my view, but wouldn’t be a problem per se (more of my thoughts on this later in the article). The real impact of AI on the economy would be as a function of productivity increasing by several orders of magnitude.

The chart below outlines GDP growth rates for the U.S. for the past 155 years. Growth rates have historically peaked either following wars (an effect of reconstruction) or following technological disruptions that increased productivity.

In the late 1800s and early 1900s, growth rates routinely reached peaks of 10%+ YoY, while not a single year of double digit growth has been seen in the US since the early 1950s.

Now, I think that with the advent of AGI we are likely to see one of two scenarios when it comes to productivity. Option 1 is that we will simply see a return to the growth rates last seen in the late industrial revolution (in the low double digits, i.e., ~10%+ per year).

Option 2 is that we are going to see an increase in productivity (and GDP growth) to a completely different order of magnitude.

Let’s talk orders of magnitude for AGI growth

Consider the below chart, which puts into historical context global GDP growth figures. For the majority of the last 2000 years, humanity has grown at near-zero growth rates.

At the global level, economic growth started increasing to sub-1% in the 18th century as a result of the first industrial revolution. It then accelerated to reach a peak of just below 3% (annualized growth rate) in the third quarter of the 20th century.

AGI, in my view, has the potential to impact the global economy to an extent similar to the second industrial revolution. In other words, it has the potential to bring growth rates to mid- to high- double digits, representing an increase of at least an order of magnitude against what we are currently “used” to. For countries like the U.S., which have historically seen growth rates far higher than the global average, I am talking about growth rates of 20%+ to 50%+, at least in the first, more disruptive, years of AGI.

AGI impact on equity markets: a secular tailwind

There is no doubt in my mind that equity markets overall will benefit from AGI as a secular tailwind. Whether option 1 or option 2 happens, stock markets reflect the state of the economy long term. And when the economy is experiencing good growth, the stock market follows.

The problem for investors is that I think there will be winners and losers from the AI race. This is a concept I have explained in detail in my past coverage, discussing to what extent the narrative of a “SaaSpocalypse” is true. To avoid writing a very lengthy article, I won’t repeat my full thesis on who I think is “winning” the AI race.

For the sake of this piece, readers should be aware that picking winners in the AI race is very difficult, as even the most resilient companies today may be at risk of losing their “moat” if they do not adopt AI effectively.

In this context, investors have two choices:

  1. They can decide to “pick and choose” AI winners, taking a high-risk, high-reward approach.

  2. They can stay invested in the broader market, benefitting from the macro tailwind (and maybe using leverage, to some extent).

Obviously, the sensible approach is the latter. But, is there another way? I think Nvidia, as one of the foundational AI companies, may be an alternative option.

AGI impact on GPU companies like Nvidia: accelerated growth

Back in 2024, Nvidia explained how they saw the AI race developing, presenting the below chart.

A way to think about this development, which I covered in my past work, is in four phases:

  • A “Picks & Shovels” phase, where hardware providers like Nvidia itself are at the center, providing the hardware needed to power data centers and train models.

  • A “Cloud and Early Adopters” phase, where cloud players see growth from early AI applications (e.g., advertising) and cloud computing needed to train AI models.

  • An “Agentic AI” or “Application Layer” phase, which in my opinion we are now in, as I described in previous sections.

  • A “Robotics” or “Physical AI” phase, where AI takes on physical tasks.

These AI phases are not sequential or mutually exclusive. Each new wave feeds demand back into the one before it. As agentic AI and robotics scale up, the need for underlying compute infrastructure does not plateau. It accelerates.

Why Nvidia is a good bet in the era of AGI

Acceleration in the need for underlying compute infrastructure is why I think Nvidia is a relatively safe bet in the era of AGI. Each new AI application requires different and more demanding chips:

  • Physical and autonomous AI needs chips optimized for real-time edge inference.

  • Edge AI demands specialized, power-efficient processors for local model execution.

  • Frontier model development continues to require the most powerful clusters.

This is why, in my view, GPU demand will not only persist but accelerate. Nvidia, as the largest AI microchip manufacturer, already offers solutions suitable for all cases. Betting on it long term makes sense, if one is to believe AGI is within reach and will impact the economy as I described.

Nvidia’s valuation is attractive today

Let me address the elephant in the room right away: yes, Nvidia’s growth rates have somewhat subsided in the last few years, as the chart below shows.

I believe this relative slowdown is the reason why Nvidia’s stock has been stagnating for the past few months, even as the company is still up 45% in the past year as of the time of writing. The market is, in my opinion, worried about the possibility that the AI race is reaching a stall.

Personally, I think the reason for this (relative) slowdown has to do with companies entering the agentic AI phase. If AI applications do not start having a real impact on businesses at scale, it is reasonable to believe AI CapEx investment (impacting demand for Nvidia’s chips) will eventually stall.

But AI applications are, and increasingly will, in my view, start having an impact on businesses. That impact will be a result of all the tools (FDEs, Perplexity Computer, Google Project Mariner, etc.) that companies are now deploying in the AI application layer. Not all companies will figure out AI at the same pace, but I believe enough AI disruption is coming at the macro level to justify an eventual increase in productivity and justify CapEx investment.

With this view, I find Nvidia’s current valuation, at a ~21X P/E more than reasonable. Nvidia has historically traded at a forward P/E ratio closer to 30 than 20, at least during the AI-driven bull markets of the past few years.

Buy and own Nvidia; don’t trade it

When covering single stocks, I always try to avoid giving precise timelines and targets. I never claim I can time the market, but rather cover stocks that I believe will be winners over a long time frame. I only assign a bullish rating to stocks that I would own for at least 10 years (and with many of them, I actually do).

Nvidia is no exception. That said, I still decided to run a simple DCF model to assess what upside may be in the stock from its current price of ~$171 (at the time of writing). Assumptions of the 10-year valuation model are as below:

  • Year 1 revenue growth of 67% (FY2027)

  • Year 2 revenue of 55%

  • A slow growth deceleration profile to 7% YoY by Year 10

  • A 10-year CAGR revenue of 27.2%, which would still be far inferior to the CAGR of Nvidia from 2020 to 2025 (60%+).

  • FCF Margin Trend of 41%, roughly in line with today

  • A conservative WACC of 14%, accounting for higher rates for longer and a risk-free rate of 4.35%.

The investment thesis behind such a valuation obviously assumes a bullish case where AI demand re-accelerates, Nvidia expands into robotics / software and its dominance persists.

The result of such a DCF model is $208 per share, representing a 22% upside from current levels. I believe the risk-reward at Nvidia’s price of $171 at the time of writing is attractive for long-term holders. The stock requires above average execution to justify its current price, but Nvidia has consistently delivered above-average execution since the AI era began. This is why I rate the stock a buy.

The upside of such a valuation model may seem limited, but if AGI is truly within reach, as I argued earlier in this article, the Bull case may prove conservative. Furthermore, my model relies on somewhat strict and conservative assumptions, such as that we are not going to see lower interest rates and accommodating monetary policy in the foreseeable future.

About UBI and the Iran war

While not strictly relevant to an investment in Nvidia, I still want to spend a few words on how AGI may impact the job market and why I think the Iran war will end up being nothing more than a buying opportunity in the long term.

For what concerns AI-driven job disruption, I am of the opinion that UBI (Universal Basic Income) is both inevitable and economically sustainable. As long as productivity increases, whether it is humans or “robots” doing the work doesn’t matter. Distributing income to unemployed masses would actually not create inflation as long as economic output doesn’t decline. I also believe that UBI is likely to come in a monetary, rather than fiscal, form. I personally see it as the natural extension of other forms of “helicopter money,” such as Quantitative Easing.

For what concerns the war in Iran, I am personally fairly worried about it for the short and mid term - something I recently openly discussed, arguing we could see oil reaching $200+ per barrel. However, I also think that it will be irrelevant long term, in the context of an AGI-driven productivity increase.

The global economy is ever-less dependent on oil, as the chart below, measuring oil intensity, shows. Oil intensity is how much oil is needed to create $1000 of global GDP.

Oil intensity has already declined by 61% since its 1973 peak. I think an ever more productive AGI world may accelerate this trend, further reducing future disruptions from geopolitical tensions. Oil is a fundamentally obsolete form of energy, with physicists broadly agreeing that, on a long enough timeline, extracting energy from our star is the most efficient path for an advanced civilization, a concept formalized in the Kardashev Scale and the physics of Dyson spheres.

I understand that may sound fairly sci-fi given the current situation, but I do firmly believe AGI can accelerate exponentially economic trends that are already in place, and I am in no way suggesting we need to wait for a Dyson sphere to be developed for the market to recover from the situation in Iran. This is also all regardless of the exact timeline we are likely to see for de-escalation.

Risks to my thesis

The main risk to investing in Nvidia today has to do with the trajectory of the AI race. My bullish take assumes we are on the verge of “achieving” AGI (whether sentient or not), as Nvidia’s CEO recently argued. The main risk is that AGI may never actually be reached to an extent that it significantly increases global productivity. Readers that are skeptical of AI may, of course, be better off not being invested in Nvidia or most tech stocks.

Another layer of risk has to do with competition. Nvidia is, today, the undisputed leader in AI microchips by revenue and margins. The main risk of being in such a position is that of disruption by other players, such as Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), a company I am personally bullish on and I see as an equally good bet to Nvidia from an investment standpoint. If competitors close their gap with Nvidia’s chips in terms of efficiency and performance, Nvidia may never benefit from the kind of growth rates I assumed in my valuation model, and may plateau.

Finally, there are general risks related to volatility and macroeconomic uncertainty. The market has been punishing Tech stocks for the best part of 2026 so far. This trend may continue, depending on how AI evolves, what companies may be disrupted by it, and the overall macro situation.

data.title: Arlo: Poised To Keep Rallying As Subscribers Grow
data.description:
The NASDAQ is now officially in correction territory, and the rest of the major indices aren’t too far behind. As war rages on in the Middle East and pushes up oil prices, investors seem to be looking for any and all reasons to sell stocks.

This is the best time, in my view, to keep a cool head and deploy active portfolio management, in particular by increasing our allocation to beaten-down smaller-cap stocks that have more rebound swing potential. Arlo (ARLO), the maker of home security cameras, looks like an especially attractive play after falling slightly this year despite strong results.

I last wrote a buy article on Arlo in January, when the stock was trading closer to $14 per share. Since then, Arlo has managed to hang on to its value relatively better than many other small cap names. At the same time, it deserves gains fundamentally: ARR growth is soaring, subscription gross margins are feeding meaningful EBITDA and cash flow growth, and the company has disciplined inventory management after an apparent quarter of strong holiday sell through. I reiterate my buy rating here.

As a reminder for the investors who are newer to Arlo, here is what I view to be the long-term bull case drivers for the company:

  • Sticky subscriber base and greenfield market for expansion. Arlo is continuing to grow paid subscribers and total subscription ARR at north of a >20% y/y clip. It has a "long range plan" that it expects to achieve by 2030 that calls for 10 million paid subs, versus 5.7 million as of the end of FY25.
  • Broad TAM. The company estimates its total addressable market in the U.S. alone is $25 billion, with a global market that is 2-3x that scale. Compared to the company's current ~$0.5 billion annual revenue scale, the company still has only low penetration into its industry.
  • Improving margin profile. Arlo is utilizing a common playbook in the consumer tech industry: "subsidizing" its subscription business with lower-cost hardware to get subscribers in the door, similar to what Roku (ROKU) and Peloton (PTON) do. And yet as subscription revenue becomes a larger part of Arlo's revenue base, the company's overall gross margin profile is also improving and unlocking meaningful EBITDA and FCF expansion.
  • Share repurchases. The company recently announced a fresh $50 million share buyback plan (worth about ~3.5% of the current market cap), which signals confidence in not only the company's ability to generate excess cash, but in the undervaluation of the stock as well.

Meanwhile, value remains one of the top reasons to buy Arlo. At current share prices near $13, Arlo trades at a market cap of $1.44 billion. After we net off the $178.9 million of cash (against zero debt) on the company's latest balance sheet, Arlo's resulting enterprise value is $1.26 billion.

As shown in the chart above, Arlo is guiding to $550-$580 million in revenue for FY26, which represents 4-10% y/y growth. Meanwhile, EPS is expected to grow to a midpoint of $0.80, which represents 14% EPS growth. And if we conservatively assume Arlo grows FCF at the same pace as revenue in FY26 (at the same 12.6% margin as in FY25), FY26 FCF would be $71.2 million (+6% y/y).

This positions Arlo's valuation multiples at:

  • 16.9x FY26 P/E
  • 14.8x FY26 ex-cash P/E
  • 17.8x EV/FY26 FCF

For a company that is still achieving >20% subscription revenue growth, is improving its gross margin stride, and has significant expansion opportunity into a broad U.S. as well as global TAM, these are more than reasonable multiples to pay. Stay long here and keep banking on further upside ahead.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Arlo's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Arlo's revenue grew 16% y/y to $141.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $135.6 million (+11% y/y) by an impressive five-point margin. Underlying subscription and services revenue, meanwhile, grew at an even faster 39% y/y pace. Hardware revenue declined; however, that's to be expected as the company came off a launch quarter in Q3.

The chart below showcases Arlo's growth in subscribers. In Q4, the company added 291k net-new paid subscribers, ending the quarter at 5.69 million paid subs. Arlo's ARR, meanwhile, grew 28% y/y to $330.5 million.

Given hardware revenue in FY25 was $213 million, even if Arlo achieves just ~flat hardware revenue in FY26, the current ARR base alone gets Arlo to the low end of the company's $550-$580 million revenue range this year.

What I find additionally impressive about Arlo's sales results in Q4 is the fact that the company continues to drain down channel inventory. As shown in the chart below, weeks of supply in the U.S. retail channel reduced to 10.1 weeks in Q4, versus 12.5 weeks in each of the previous two quarters. Similarly, U.S. distribution channel was reduced to just 3.0 weeks of supply, down from 5.5 weeks in Q3 and 11 weeks in Q2.

These relatively low post-holiday sales inventory figures indicate that Arlo is due for restocking orders, particularly from U.S. distributors.

Management noted that customers' reaction to newly launched products remained strong, while subscriber churn dropped to new lows. Per CEO Matthew McRae's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

The reception of our new products and services has been outstanding. Our customer and professional reviews are the strongest Arlo has seen for a new product launch in our history with numerous models already receiving multiple editor's choice and best of awards. Our new lineup not only contributed to Q4 results, it serves as the foundation of our continuing growth in 2026.

The other example of exceptional execution is when you look across our SaaS performance metrics. Our monthly consolidated churn dropped to 1% in the fourth quarter. Or said another way, our monthly subscriber retention rate is 99%, which means a paying user stays with our service for more than 8 years on average. This achievement is the culmination of numerous small improvements across our platform, including performance enhancements, customer care improvements, billing system improvements and deeper insights at the user cohort level driven by our vast data sets."

Profitability also continued to scale meaningfully. Arlo's adjusted EBITDA in Q4 grew more than 2x y/y to $23.3 million, reflecting a 16.5% margin that improved 850bps y/y.

On top of this, Arlo hit $66.9 million in FCF in FY25, at a 12.6% margin that improved 310bps y/y.

Key takeaways

In my view, Arlo remains a heavily overlooked stock that has very attractive fundamentals. The company continues to attract a raft of new subscribers while substantially expanding its ARR, while recent product launches are showcasing strong sell-through, as evidenced by sequential declines in channel inventory. With Arlo trading at mid-teens multiples of earnings and FCF, this remains a ripe buying opportunity.

data.title: Kingfisher: Green Shoots Likely To Be Cut Off
data.description:
Introduction

Kingfisher (KGFHF) (KGFHY) is a home improvement retailer operating in the UK, Ireland, France, Poland, and elsewhere and selling into both the DIY and trade businesses. Its brands include B&Q, Screwfix, Castorama and Brico Dépôt. It is the number one such retailer in the UK and number two in France.

The company’s fortunes have been a mixed bag over the last few years, reflected in total growth in adjusted EPS of only 8.2% in the ten years from FY16 to the recently reported FY26. This may come as a surprise to investors familiar with the equivalent US companies in this space, Home Depot (HD) and Lowe’s (LOW), which have respectively grown EPS by 161% and 334% in the same period. However, while there are many similarities between the sector in the US and Kingfisher, both end demand and the competitive landscape are different.

Recently reported results reflect modest signs of consumer willingness to spend in the UK and also the steady impact of company efforts to emphasize the professional channel and e-commerce, including third party sales to leverage the company’s online presence. While all of this is encouraging, I believe the shares are already pricing this in and that the economic impact of the fighting in Iran will weigh on results for this year. I rate the shares Hold.

Why Kingfisher is a little different from Home Depot

In my opinion, it's worth briefly comparing and contrasting Kingfisher with the US duopoly to give some context, before then discussing each of the main segments and why I believe the shares will struggle to perform for the time being.

At first glance, the similarities between Kingfisher’s core business and Home Depot and Lowe’s seem overwhelming. After all, Kingfisher principally uses large format stores in developed markets to sell into both the home improvement DIY and Do-It-For-Me (i.e. trade) markets. So far, so familiar. However, there are some key characteristics which differ and make life rather harder for Kingfisher.

In the case of both Kingfisher and Lowe's, it is true that their respective revenues have fluctuated to some extent as a result of the pandemic and the aftermath. For example, Lowe’s saw a surge and a peak of $97 billion in the year to 31 January 2023, followed by a decline to $86.3 billion in FY25 just reported. In a similar way, Kingfisher’s revenues had a peak in its FY22 at £13.2bn, with FY26 revenues of £12.9bn.

By way of contrast, Home Depot has actually already managed to exceed the pandemic peak in sales, reporting $164.7 billion in the year to 31 January 2026, up 4.6% in three years. I believe this reflects its heavier and growing exposure to the ‘pro’ market and meaningful M&A to that end.

But when we look at the longer-term margin performance for all three companies and include data for Kingfisher’s key competitor in the UK, Wickes (LSE: WIX) since it has been a public company (flotation January 2021), we can see a structural difference in the two countries’ markets:

As you can see, on the one hand, over the long-term, the UK retailers have had gross margins consistently 3-4% higher than their US peers. But conversely, the US duopoly have had much more controlled SG&A as a percentage of sales, and to a striking extent. The bottom line is that EBIT margins for the American pair are currently 11-13%, compared to 4-5.5% in the UK.

The reasons for this are varied. The ‘potted summary’ is that across multiple Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), the US pair outperform the UK companies as a result of innate advantages. In my view, these include (but are not limited to):

  • Higher sales per square foot from higher throughput in generally bigger stores
  • Higher operating leverage from scale
  • Logistical advantages from very large centralized distribution centers.
  • A more fragmented market in the UK and France (discussed below)
  • Much stronger GDP growth in the US in recent years
  • The fact that Kingfisher operates under multiple brands targeted at different customers and in different jurisdictions, adding complexity and overhead.

In my view, these many comparative advantages help explain the ability of the American pair to grow market share and profitably expand.

The UK business is the crown jewel

The UK housing market was steady to positive in 2025, helping household goods sales, in contrast to many other retail categories:

This has been helpful for Kingfisher, which, via its B&Q (59% of UK sales in FY26) and Screwfix (41% sales) channels, is the clear number one player in this home improvement market. By one estimate, the total UK home improvement market was worth over £29bn in 2024, which would put Kingfisher at a share of about 22%.

As seen in the snapshot at the beginning, the UK division earns the lion’s share of overall company profit. FY26 sales rose 4.2%, with same store sales up 3.3%. Overall retail profit was up 3%, driven by big-ticket items, solid core products, a little help from weather, and some traffic gains from problems at competitor Homebase (more on that in a moment). Management commented that they believe they have taken market share.

Trade as a percentage of sales has reached 30% in the UK, up 3% in just one year, from a productive decision to shift more towards the professional channel in recent years. This mirrors the strategy adopted by Home Depot. A further tailwind is the growth in their online presence, with e-commerce sales representing 21% of group sales in FY26, up 11% from FY25. Within e-commerce, the company is successfully leveraging its platform to sell margin-accretive third-party sales, which now account for 15% of e-commerce (up from 10% in FY25).

Perhaps unsurprisingly, the company’s fortunes are consistent with evidence from Google Trends. For example, this five-year chart shows search trends in the UK for ‘bathroom renovation’:

These are obviously modestly positive market trends, and you will see a contrast with France below. However, as I will discuss, I can’t help but believe that recent geopolitical events are likely to derail this momentum for at least this calendar year.

As mentioned earlier, B&Q has benefited slightly in the last fiscal year from the demise of Homebase. This business had been troubled for years, as detailed here. Arguably, its troubled history illustrates the challenges inherent to the UK home improvement and furnishing market, notably the importance of adaptability and cost control. This serves to emphasize how well Kingfisher has done on a relative basis in the UK.

France is a drag, but may have bottomed

Kingfisher’s presence in France is via the similarly-sized Castorama (broadline home improvement) and Brico Dépôt (value and trade focus) is the #2 presence in broad home improvement in France at around 25% market share, with leader Leroy Merlin the clear number one at 39%.

FY26 results noted an overall French market decline of 3% in FY26, with Kingfisher itself recording same-store sales of -2.2% and -2.3% for Castorama and Brico Dépôt respectively. The company noted in its recent press release that the French market has declined by 10% in just two years. Although it is targeting an improvement in operating margin from 2.5% to over 5% in the medium-term, clearly overall demand is going to be important for any progress.

Google Trends is once again consistent with these challenges. Here are searches for ‘cuisine aménagée’ (fitted kitchen) since 2021:

Poland has potential but isn’t immune from market softness

Kingfisher’s presence here is again through the Castorama brand, and again Leroy Merlin is its biggest competitor. These two companies account for slightly over half of the DIY market.

While operating margins are not as depressed as in France, at 4.7% in FY26, they are still well below the UK’s 8.5%. A flat market in FY26 was reflected in SSS of -1.1%.

After strong house price appreciation, inflationary pressures and the high cost of housing have been impediments to affordability for renovation spending. And complications from the end of a mortgage subsidy scheme haven’t helped either. So while trend GDP growth and rising living standards suggest great potential, right now home improvement is flatlining.

The UK will be particularly affected by the war in the Gulf

The broader global impact of the current conflict involving the US, Israel, and Iran, of course, has all sorts of ramifications. I would argue that the UK has particular vulnerability from an economic point of view and that this is a material issue for Kingfisher.

For example, 85% of the population uses natural gas for home heating. This has three principal sources: the UK’s own North Sea reserves, Norway (also North Sea) and LNG imports. I believe that the global market for this last category is likely to tighten significantly next winter owing to the recent substantial damage to Qatari LNG facilities. Even in a best-case scenario of an early reopening of the Straits of Hormuz, in my view this global LNG pricing impact will be an overhang for this calendar year at the very least.

More broadly, the UK population has been wrestling with cost-of-living pressures for several years now post-pandemic, and consumer confidence is fragile. Taxes are at a peace-time high and economic growth had already slowed to a crawl prior to the Tehran bombing, which sparked hostilities. There is even growing evidence of the population adopting a strategy of a higher savings rate going forward compared to before the pandemic, although this has softened a little in the most recent statistics. While LNG demand will be low in the summer, in my opinion household heating costs will be one of the main coming sentiment headwinds.

With regard to the oil price spike, gasoline prices are harder to predict, as an early resolution may limit the damage. However, the UK already has some of the highest energy costs in the world for industry and households, across both heating and electricity. This means that even a one-off shock, as it ripples through the supply chain, is in my view likely to spark morale-sapping inflation right when relief finally seemed on the horizon.

In this context, I believe there is a high risk of a meaningful retrenchment in UK consumer confidence and, crucially, a somewhat sustained increase in interest rates as the central bank seeks to limit inflationary pressures. Indeed, UK government bonds (aka gilts) have already seen sharp yield increases in anticipation. I just can’t see an appetite for substantial home renovation work in the UK this year in this environment.

The current valuation isn’t appealing given 2026 headwinds

The company’s shares trade on around 11.1x January 27 (FY27) consensus eps of 26p. We can get a sense of the embedded assumptions within that consensus eps number from the data provider itself. As at 12 February 2026 (i.e. before final results reported 24 March), FY27 group sales are expected to be up 2.2% compared to now-reported FY26. Within this, UK SSS are forecasted at +1.8%, France 0% and Poland 1.8%. And so the valuation is using numbers that should now be somewhat doubtful, in my view.

As you can see from the long-term forward P/E chart, even after the drop so far this month, the forward multiple is still towards the upper end of the historic range:

In my opinion, such a valuation compared to long-term history doesn't really allow for the growing risk of downward revisions due principally from top-line softening versus consensus expectations.

Risks – where I could be wrong (to the up and downside)

If I’m too pessimistic about the mood of UK consumers in 2026, home improvement spending could surprise to the upside and this would probably drive the stock higher.

Also, there have been suggestions in the past, like this one from 2013, that Kingfisher would be an appetizing takeover target for a big overseas retailer looking to rapidly build a UK home improvement presence. However, I don’t think this is likely any time soon. This is partly because of the structural challenges outlined earlier. But also because the Homebase debacle mentioned above has highlighted the challenges for would-be new entrants. However, I could be wrong about this.

The principal risk in terms of severe downside is a deep and prolonged UK recession.

Summary

So overall, while management’s actions to steer the core business more towards the professional market and to leverage the digital offering make a great deal of sense, I see the shares as relatively fairly valued for the time being and recommend a Hold. All else held equal, if the shares were to fall meaningfully from here, say to a 9x forward multiple, implying roughly 20% downside, I would reconsider my rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Stable Cash Flows, Growth Potential, And Strong Performance (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD) is a midstream master limited partnership that owns and operates a network of crude oil, natural gas, natural gas liquids, and refined products transportation infrastructure that covers much of the continental United States. The company is one of the largest midstream master limited partnerships in the nation, and it is one of the most popular ones among investors on Seeking Alpha. One possible reason for this is that the company has a relatively high 5.63% distribution yield and a history of maintaining or growing its distribution over time. In fact, Enterprise Products Partners was one of the only midstream partnerships that did not cut its distributions in the aftermath of the 2014 oil bear market or the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic. Furthermore, the company’s distributions are tax-advantaged, which may be a very attractive characteristic for investors who happen to be in a high tax bracket. Furthermore, the company's units are likely to outperform as long as the hostilities in Iran continue to disrupt shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and destroy energy infrastructure throughout the Middle East. As such, Enterprise Products Partners may be worth considering for a portfolio today.

Thesis Summary

Oil prices have risen substantially since the outbreak of hostilities between Iran, Israel, and the United States. This is largely due to the Iranians disrupting shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway through which 20% to 25% of all oil consumed worldwide flows. It is unclear when shipping traffic through this region will be normalized, but it is unlikely that oil prices will decline immediately even once it is. This conclusion is due to the fact that Iran has been using missiles and drones to destroy the energy infrastructure of other Middle Eastern states, and it will take a long time to rebuild this infrastructure. Enterprise Products Partners could be very well positioned to deliver growth should oil-importing nations turn to the United States to diversify their suppliers or replace the oil that is no longer coming out of the Middle East.

About Enterprise Products Partners

As stated in the introduction, Enterprise Products Partners is a midstream master limited partnership that owns a network of hydrocarbon transportation infrastructure that stretches across much of the continental United States:

In total, Enterprise Products Partners owns approximately 50,000 miles of pipelines, 300 million barrels of hydrocarbon liquids storage, and 21 deepwater docks that are designed to handle ocean-going ships that transport energy products into and out of the United States.

Enterprise Products Partners operates more than just pipeline infrastructure, however. The company is also one of the largest processors of natural gas and natural gas liquids in the United States. The company owns 45 natural gas processing trains as well as 27 natural gas liquids fractionators. These two pieces of infrastructure provide services that are vital to the natural gas value chain in the United States:

  • Natural Gas Processing Trains: Natural gas does not come out of the ground in a form that is usable by most natural gas appliances, as it contains a number of impurities as well as non-methane hydrocarbons (such as ethane, propane, butane, and other substances). Natural gas processing trains remove these impurities and non-methane hydrocarbons and convert the product into pipeline-quality dry natural gas. It is this pipeline-quality dry natural gas that is ultimately consumed by end-users of the substance.
  • Natural Gas Liquids Fractionators: A natural gas liquids stream includes a variety of hydrocarbon substances, including butane, propane, ethane, isobutane, and pentanes. A natural gas liquids fractionator separates each of these different substances out of the natural gas liquids stream so that they can be sold and utilized separately.

As we can see, Enterprise Products Partners provides a number of services that are important to the oil and gas industry. Naturally, the company earns money from each of the services that it performs. Over the past twelve months, Enterprise Products Partners reported total revenue of $52.5960 billion, a slight decline from 2024’s levels and slightly below what the company brought in during 2022, but otherwise it was one of the highest revenue figures that Enterprise Products Partners has ever reported:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

One thing that we do notice here is that Enterprise Products Partners’ revenues do appear to exhibit a slight correlation with energy prices. In particular, we see that its revenues declined in 2020 alongside the COVID-19-driven collapse in energy prices. We also see that the company’s revenues came in the highest during 2022, which had the highest average energy prices of any of the years shown. This is because some of the company’s business segments are margin-based, much like a refinery. Basically, Enterprise Products Partners buys the raw feedstocks (the unprocessed products that came out of the ground) and then sells the processed products at a higher price. This is the reason why we see the company’s cost of revenues (cost of goods sold) also exhibiting a correlation with energy prices:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

We can see that Enterprise Products Partners’ cost of revenue came in at $45.390 billion for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025. This was down a bit from 2024, just like the company’s revenues were. In addition, the company had the highest cost of revenues in 2022, which also happened to be the year in which both energy prices and Enterprise Products Partners’ revenues were the highest. By the same token, the company’s cost of revenues dropped substantially in 2020 alongside the steep decline in energy prices.

Ultimately, then, we can see that there is a correlation between Enterprise Products Partners’ revenues and its cost of revenues. This actually results in the company’s gross profits being relatively stable over time:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

As we can see, the company’s gross profits steadily grew over the nine-year period shown in the chart. The gross profits did not appear to exhibit much correlation with energy prices, unlike both revenue and cost of goods sold. This is something that will likely prove to be fairly attractive to most income-seeking investors. After all, the majority of investors for whom income is their highest priority are retirees or other individuals who are somewhat risk-averse. Ultimately, it is gross profit that the company uses to cover its operating expenses and provide a distribution to its shareholders. As such, the stability of the company’s gross profits seems likely to appeal to such individuals since it should give the company a relatively stable financial base.

Indeed, we do see that Enterprise Products Partners has a relatively stable cash flow. This chart shows the company’s net cash flow provided by operating activities over the 2017 to 2025 period:

(figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

As this chart shows 2025’s breakdown by quarter, it might be a little bit hard to follow. Therefore, here is the same chart showing only the full-year figures:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

We can see that the company’s cash flows were generally stable over the period, or at least far more stable than energy prices. In addition, we can see that the company’s cash flows tended to exhibit a steady rise over the period. This is very good for investors who are seeking to earn an income from their portfolios because it is cash flow that ultimately determines the amount of money that a given company can pay out to its investors. As this figure has grown over time, it lends itself well to distribution growth. As it is quite possible that inflation will continue to be a problem for the near future, we would want the company to increase its distribution so that the income that we receive from Enterprise Products Partners retains its value in real terms.

Speaking of which, if we assume that the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers is an accurate measurement of inflation, then Enterprise Products Partners has grown its adjusted cash from operations at a pace that was higher than inflation over the period. According to that index, $4.6341 billion in December 2016 (the last month before EPD’s 2017 fiscal year began) is equivalent in real terms to $6.27238 billion today. Enterprise Products Partners’ most recently reported twelve-month cash flow figures are higher than that amount. As such, the company grew its operating cash flow at a pace exceeding inflation over the nine-year period. This is also a very nice thing for any investor who is depending on their assets and portfolios to support themselves.

Enterprise Products Partners has used its cash flow growth over the nine-year period to reward its unitholders with a steadily growing distribution. Over the course of 2017, Enterprise Products Partners paid out distributions totaling $1.6675 per common unit:

This distribution increased to $2.16 per common unit over the course of 2025:

While distribution increases are nice, this is somewhat discouraging from the perspective of someone who is trying to live off of their portfolio. According to the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers, a distribution of $1.67 in December 2016 would need to be $2.24 today in order to have the same purchasing power. We can see that Enterprise Products Partners did not manage to increase its distribution by enough to meet this requirement over the period. Even if we start in December 2017, the company’s distribution has not kept up with inflation. Thus, it would have been necessary to use some of the distributions that Enterprise Products Partners paid out over the last nine years to purchase additional common units of the company in order to ensure that an investor’s income from the company kept up with inflation over the period. This is somewhat disappointing, as it means that the amount of spendable income that investors receive from this company is less than the 5.63% yield would suggest.

Enterprise Products Partners Versus Peer Midstream Partnerships

As mentioned earlier in this article, Enterprise Products Partners is one of the largest midstream master limited partnerships in the United States. As of the time of writing, the company has a market capitalization of $84.52 billion and an enterprise value of $119.31 billion. Here is how this compares to some of the other midstream master limited partnerships in the market:

Partnership Name

Market Capitalization

Enterprise Products Partners

We can see here that, in terms of market capitalization, Enterprise Products Partners is the largest midstream master limited partnership in the United States. The company is not the largest in terms of enterprise value, however, which is due to the fact that Energy Transfer has a much higher level of debt. As of December 31, 2025, Enterprise Products Partners only has $33.9130 billion in net debt compared to $68.8220 billion in net debt for Energy Transfer. Enterprise value is equal to market capitalization plus net debt, so Energy Transfer’s higher level of debt results in that company actually having a higher enterprise value despite its lower market capitalization relative to EPD.

Enterprise Products Partners is also larger than many midstream C-corporations in terms of both market capitalization and enterprise value. We can see that in this chart:

We can see that, in general, the midstream C-corporations shown above are larger than the master limited partnerships that were shown in the previous paragraph. This is not particularly surprising, as the valuations of many C-corporations are higher than those of comparable midstream master limited partnerships. We can see that by looking at the forward price-to-earnings ratios of the above companies:

As shown, all of the master limited partnerships have forward price-to-earnings ratios of less than 15, whereas the C-corporations have ratios that are in the 20s or 30s. Thus, the C-corporations clearly have higher valuations. This impacts their relative enterprise values due to the fact that the more highly valued a company is, the higher its market capitalization. Market capitalization is an input into the enterprise value formula. Thus, the C-corporations appear to be larger companies in terms of market capitalization and enterprise value, but they may not necessarily be larger companies in terms of things such as cash flow or profitability. In other words, the master limited partnerships, including Enterprise Products Partners, could provide an investor with more cash flow per dollar invested in the company than the C-corporations will.

This also extends to their yields, as the master limited partnerships shown above offer substantially higher yields:

Enterprise Products Partners is the lowest-yielding of the master limited partnerships, but its yield is still higher than that possessed by any of the C-corporations. This is consistent with the generally lower valuations of the master limited partnerships, as investors in these companies have to pay less for each dollar of earnings or cash flow produced by the master limited partnerships when compared to the C-corporations. This is something that could be attractive for investors whose goal is to maximize the income that they receive from their portfolios. For the most part, choosing a master limited partnership will provide a higher level of income and generate more cash flow than choosing any of the C-corporations.

Another advantage for a master limited partnership, such as Enterprise Products Partners, over a C-corporation is its tax efficiency. Most master limited partnerships do not have to pay federal income taxes on a company level. We can immediately see this when comparing the finances of Enterprise Products Partners to those of a C-corporation such as Kinder Morgan. For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, Enterprise Products Partners reported earnings before taxes of $5.8990 billion, but only had a tax liability of $23.0 million:

That is an effective tax rate of 0.390%, well below the statutory federal corporate income tax rate. Kinder Morgan, on the other hand, had a lower reported earnings before taxes of $3.992 billion for the full-year 2025 period, but it had to pay $832.0 million in taxes:

That is a 20.84% effective tax rate, far higher than Enterprise Products Partners had to pay. While there are undeniably factors that can affect the rate that a given company ultimately pays, the takeaway here is that master limited partnerships have much lower effective rates of taxation than do C-corporations. This allows a master limited partnership to have more money available to reward its common equity holders, all else being equal.

Investors in master limited partnerships also get tax benefits that result in the actual amount of spendable income provided by these companies being higher relative to the C-corporations than their yields would suggest. This tax benefit comes in the fact that distributions paid out by a master limited partnership like Enterprise Products Partners are considered to be a return of capital and simply reduce an investor’s cost basis in the company’s equity. Investors in Enterprise Products Partners do not have to pay taxes on the distributions that they receive from the company until their cost basis is reduced to zero (in which the distributions are taxed as long-term capital gains) or they sell their units at a price that is greater than their cost basis in the company (adjusted for the distributions). This means that an investor who is willing to buy the company’s units and hold them will not have to pay taxes on most of the income that they receive from the company for the first several years of ownership, which can be a very appealing characteristic for any investor in a high-income tax bracket. Clearly, this results in an investor receiving more after-tax income from a master limited partnership than they would from a C-corporation with the same yield and a requirement to pay current-year taxes on the dividends that it pays out.

Please note, however, that holding units of a master limited partnership inside of a tax-advantaged account (like most 401k or IRA accounts) can actually result in an investor having to pay current-year taxes on distributions that their IRA received. This is something known as unrelated business taxable income. According to Legal Clarity, the everyday operations of a master limited partnership (including Enterprise Products Partners) are considered active business income, which is unrelated to the tax-exempt purpose of a retirement account and therefore causes the IRA itself to have tax liabilities. As such, it is best to hold units of Enterprise Products Partners in an ordinary brokerage account rather than in some sort of tax-exempt vehicle.

Natural Gas Liquids Focus And Growth Opportunities

Unlike many other pipeline operators in the United States, Enterprise Products Partners generates the majority of its profits and cash flow from the transportation and fractionation of natural gas liquids. For the full-year 2025 period, Enterprise Products Partners reported a total segment gross operating margin of $10 billion. Of that amount, 55% came from performing various services involving natural gas liquids:

This is different from some other midstream companies in the nation. For example, an outsized proportion of The Williams Company’s revenue and cash flow comes from natural gas. Plains All American Pipeline primarily transports crude oil and refined products. While all of these compounds are hydrocarbons, they do have different fundamentals, and this can affect the growth prospects of each company. For example, the domestic and worldwide demand for natural gas and natural gas liquids is likely to grow much more than the demand for crude oil and refined products going forward. This means that those companies that transport natural gas and natural gas liquids likely have stronger growth prospects than those companies that focus primarily on crude oil.

Enterprise Products Partners is no exception to this, as the company currently has $4.8 billion worth of major capital projects under construction. Most of these projects are intended to increase the company’s abilities to handle natural gas or natural gas liquids, which, as the chart above shows, are currently its two largest contributors to its segment gross operating margin. Here are a few of the major capital projects that Enterprise Products Partners is currently constructing:

We can actually see that many of these are scheduled to come online later this year. For example, the company has two natural gas processing plants in the Permian Basin that are scheduled to come online this year (Mentone West and Athena). Each of these two plants has a nameplate capacity of 300 million cubic feet of natural gas per day, so they are clearly intended to provide additional processing capacity for the growing amount of natural gas being extracted from the ground in the Delaware and Midland Basins (the two primary hubs of the Permian Basin). The U.S. Energy Information Administration provides the following charts showing the natural gas production in the United States by region over the January 2019 to December 2025 period:

The range at the top of the chart refers specifically to the Permian Basin, and as we can see, it gets progressively wider as we move from 2019 to 2025. This is supportive of the idea that natural gas production in the Permian Basin is increasing, which means that more natural gas processing capacity is required. After all, natural gas cannot be used by appliances straight out of the ground. It needs to be processed first, as was discussed earlier in this article. Enterprise Products Partners is taking advantage of this opportunity by constructing new natural gas processing plants to handle the growing need.

Enterprise Products Partners is also increasing its capacity for handling natural gas liquids coming out of the Permian Basin. One way through which the company is doing this is by increasing the capacity of the Bahia natural gas liquids pipeline. The Bahia pipeline is a 550-mile natural gas liquids pipeline that runs from the Delaware and Midland Basins to Enterprise Products Partners’ massive natural gas liquids fractionation facility in Mt. Belvieu, Texas. The pipeline is capable of carrying approximately 600,000 barrels of natural gas liquids per day along its route.

In mid-November, Enterprise Products Partners announced that oil and gas supermajor ExxonMobil (XOM) will be purchasing a 40% equity stake in the Bahia Pipeline for approximately $650 million. This money is essentially intended to be a reimbursement for some of the money that has already been invested into the construction of the pipeline. In addition, the companies announced that the originally planned 600,000 barrels of natural gas liquids per day capacity is no longer sufficient to meet the industry’s needs. As the production of natural gas liquids in the Permian continues to increase (current estimates state that NGL production volume will increase by 30% by 2030), the two companies have decided to increase the capacity of this pipeline to one million barrels per day. In addition to this increased capacity, the two companies will construct an extension to the Bahia pipeline system that runs to ExxonMobil’s Cowboy natural gas processing plant in Eddy County, New Mexico (also in the Permian Basin).

The fact that ExxonMobil has gotten involved in this project is something that investors in Enterprise Products Partners might find appealing. After all, ExxonMobil itself intends to become a customer of this pipeline system, and it will also be the primary customer of the extension that runs to its natural gas processing plant. As such, we can be fairly certain that the new, larger capacity is actually needed and will be contracted once the project is completed. Therefore, we can be confident that the completion of this project will provide a boost to Enterprise Products Partners’ revenue and cash flow upon completion. This is much better than a situation where the company invests a lot of money to construct infrastructure that is not needed and nobody actually wants to use.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed Enterprise Products Partners was on October 10, 2025. The company’s common units are up 26.99% since that time, substantially outperforming the S&P 500 Index over the same period:

We can see, however, that the majority of the company’s outperformance came in the past month or two. The EPD price chart first begins to trend above the chart for the S&P 500 Index in mid-January, but it really took off as we entered February and March. There are a few reasons for this, a few of which we have already discussed in various articles published to Energy Profits in Dividends over the past few weeks. The outbreak of hostilities in Iran, for example, has disrupted shipping traffic moving through the Strait of Hormuz and caused the price of crude oil to increase. That has had a positive impact on the prices of just about every equity in the energy sector, including ones that do not experience a significant impact in cash flow from rising energy prices. As we have already seen, Enterprise Products Partners does not see its cash flow affected much by rising energy prices. However, this does not mean that the company cannot benefit at all from the problems in the Middle East. For example, should oil shipments coming out of the Persian Gulf continue to be disrupted, then it is possible that purchasers of oil will begin to look for alternative sources of crude oil, one of which could be the United States. If upstream companies in the United States increase their production to satisfy this increased demand, then more pipeline and processing capacity will be needed. Enterprise Products Partners could benefit by constructing new pipelines and thus transporting more crude oil. That would grow the company’s revenues and cash flow. This scenario would, however, require that Iran continue to stop traffic from moving through the Strait of Hormuz and that nations in the Middle East cannot find alternative ways to get oil out of the region to customers in Asia. It seems somewhat unlikely that this scenario will actually play out, given that Middle Eastern countries such as Saudi Arabia have begun to use alternative ports to bypass the Strait of Hormuz and get their products onto the market, and few upstream American producers expect the hostilities to last long enough to justify increasing production.

Distribution Analysis

On January 8, 2026, Enterprise Products Partners raised its distribution to $0.55 per common unit. This was a slight increase over the $0.545 that the company was paying previously. This new distribution works out to $2.20 per common unit annualized, which represents a 5.63% yield at the current price. As is always the case, though, we should have a look at the company’s finances to see how well it can actually afford this.

For the full-year period that ended on December 31, 2025, Enterprise Products Partners reported an operational distributable cash flow of $7.904 billion. The company had 2.1618 billion shares outstanding as of December 31, 2025. Therefore, the current distribution would cost the company approximately $4.75596 billion. That means that its operating distributable cash flow should be enough to cover the distribution 1.66 times over, which is very reasonable. Wall Street analysts usually consider anything over 1.20x sustainable, and given that Enterprise Products Partners’ cash flows do not usually exhibit much volatility, we can conclude that the company should be able to afford its distribution.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Enterprise Products Partners is a very large midstream master limited partnership that has a reasonable valuation despite the recent surge in its common unit price. The company is well-positioned to profit from growth in the domestic and worldwide demand for natural gas and natural gas liquids, as it is advancing several projects that will increase its capacity for handling such products. The company also boasts a respectable, tax-advantaged 5.63% yield that appears to be sustainable. Overall, Enterprise Products Partners continues to deserve a place as a core holding in any income investor’s portfolio.
